sections.label: 1. Call the regular meeting to order. 7:00 P.M.
a. Public hearing on the Fern Street Connector Path project. (No Council action).

b. Public hearing and Council action on an ordinance amending Chapter 102 (Utilities), Article II (Fees and Charges), Section 102-33, pertaining to sanitary sewer connection fees, frontage charges and wastewater lateral repair and replacement charges, and
c. Public hearing and Council action on an ordinance adopting printed Supplement Numbers 16 to 26, inclusive, to the Code of the City of Fairfax, Virginia.

d. Public hearing and Council action on an ordinance amending Chapter 2 (Administration), Article III (Officers and Employees), Division 2 (Disclosure of Interests), pertaining to required financial disclosures by certain enumerated officers and employees

12. Council Comments and Committee reports out (if needed).

turns.text: Motion passed unanimously. We will move on to public hearing. Our first public hearing is on the Fern Street Connector Path Project, and there's no council action on this item tonight. I'm going to recognize Chloe Ritter, the multimodal transportation planner, to provide the staff presentation. Okay, good evening, Mayor and Council. We are here, this item, uh, to discuss the Fern Street connector path and have a public hearing on this. But before we get started, let's see, I would like to just briefly introduce Our consultant, Megan Waring, who is from Kimley-Horn, so she will be here helping me discuss the design of the project. And then we also have some other staff in the audience in case there are questions that we need to answer. So the purpose of the public hearing is to give some background on the project and give a design presentation. After we give the presentation, we will answer questions and invite public comment. But this is a design public hearing where no public— no City Council action is required. So we are really here just to listen and take feedback and then there will be a public comment period that goes on for another week and then we will give responses to all of the comments at the end of that period. So first, the project overview. The location of this project is the— on Fern Street just south of Route 29. This came out of the Camp Washington Small Area Plan and it is a connection between the Fern Street dead end that is just south of Route 29 and the intersection of Fern Street and Park Road just south of that. And right now that is kind of a grassy area and this project would build a pedestrian and bicycle connection to connect the commercial area with the neighborhood and activate that green space and that open space in the Camp Washington Activity Center. This is where we are on the project. We are here at Step 10. As I said, this is a Design Public Hearing for the intermediate design plans this evening. And this is where we have been on the project so far. As I said, this came out of the Camp Washington Small Area Plan which was adopted in 2022. And then the project was advanced in the 2-year transportation program. We had an open house and endorsed the funding application in 2023, and then last year we got the funding authorized and we started the design of the project, and we're here this evening for the design public hearing. And now I'm going to turn it over to Megan to discuss the design. Great, so thank you all for having me speak this evening. Um, as you see on the screen here, we have the Fern Street path design. Just to orient yourself as you're looking on the screen, We do have the project twisted to the right. So north is going to be to your right, Fern Street and Park Road. That intersection is on screen left. And the Fairfax Junction Shopping Center is going to be on screen right. This project design is a 10-foot path connector trail. It's 10 feet from the intersection of Park Road and Fern Street. You see that there's two connections that tie to the existing sidewalk that goes along Park Street today. There is a— or a new crosswalk just south on Park Road that exists today. That will be the crossing that exists in this design as well. This path is about 200 feet long. It meanders throughout that green open space that Chloe mentioned. Along the path, it has amenities similar to the Hill Street project that I know that you all are familiar with— amenities such as trash receptacles, lighting, and benches, native trees and plantings along there. We also have a final connection at Fern Street right by that shopping center entrance where we connect to both sidewalks on Fern Street itself, and we will be replacing the entrance at the shopping center to upgrade it to be ADA compliant as part of the connection of this street. Some amenities that are part of the Fern Street project that you can see here are very similar to Hill Street, and you'll see a lot of images that you're familiar with from Hill Street. So those park-like amenities with the trash receptacles, the benches, path-level lighting along there, so very similar to the Hill Street as well with those solar-level path lightings, and then those native trees along the path as well with some plantings at either entrance. You will notice at the entrance of both sides at Park and Fern and also up at the shopping center entrance, we do have lighting to show the entrance of that path. And then along the path itself. So those are the amenities that are planned as part of the project itself and they go along the path for about that 200 feet there making that closed connection. [Speaker:COMMISSIONER TREGONING] Okay. So this is a summary of the public engagement that we have done so far. Going back to the beginning of the project, there was, of course, public engagement as part of the Small Area Plan and as part of the 2-year program. Again, we had that funding open house in the summer of 2023 before we held the public hearing to endorse the funding application. Now, more recently, since we started the design of the project, we updated the information on the Engage page, and this slide was created before the holidays, so there's been a few more visitors. I think there's been about 250 visitors to the Engage page since November, and since we put out exhibits at the park, at the location to show what the exhibits would look like, and we sent out information to the community and asking them for feedback. And we've gotten about 10 comments so far, generally supportive with a variety of suggestions for modifications to some of the amenities. We notified the public in a variety of ways with A-frame signs, we mailed letters to residents immediately adjacent to the project. It was in the City Scene. We did a newspaper ad as required for our public hearing requirements and then put it out through the City email and social media accounts. And then this is the schedule looking forward. So tonight is the public hearing and then the public hearing record will be open through January 23rd. So any comments that we receive tonight as well as the comments that we have already received And then comments that we receive through January 23rd will be in the public hearing record and we will provide written responses to all of those comments. And then following that, we will develop the 100% plans and then send this out for a construction award. So that would be towards the end of this year, we would be looking to wrap up the plans and begin construction. And as far as the project budget, we do have approximately $350,000 in in transportation alternatives, 80% of that is federal money and then 20% of it is the local match from our CNI fund. And that does fund the entire project as it is budgeted right now. So that concludes our presentation. We will be happy to take any questions. Questions? [Speaker:COMMISSIONER TREGONING] All right. Are there any questions for staff on this from the dais? Commissioner Peterson.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Just a general question. The ratio of engineering to construction on cost, this is a relatively small project and so the ratio, about a third of this is engineering and two-thirds construction as I see it. Is that pretty typical for this size class and does that stretch— how much— how does that stretch across other size classes?
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: So the ratio of engineering to construction does vary by project size. So when you have a $20 million project, the percentage that's engineering is going to be a lot smaller. There are some fixed costs that are hard to scale up. There's VDOT oversight costs, and then there's just a certain amount of fixed cost in terms of survey work that is hard to scale down to a smaller size. So this is ratio-wise, a smaller project is going to have a slightly higher ratio of engineering costs.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: [Speaker:COMMISSIONER MAY] Yeah, like there are savings as far as in the plan set because there's less plan sheets, but when you have utility relocations and survey and other items where crews have to come out to the field and test, those are pretty constant. In fact, they might even be a little bit more expensive for smaller projects. So for, as Chloe said, for a large project, maybe a $10 million project, your design fee is going to be 10%, maybe 12%. For a $300,000 project, it could be 30%, 40%. It's not unusual. [Speaker:COMMISSIONER MAY] And just to follow up, and not to suggest a concern or a criticism of the project by any means, but I'm just curious about kind of this type and size class. Does this benchmark out pretty typically in terms of the engineering and construction ratio and then the scale of the engineering cost on this? Yes, it does, sir. Okay, so this is pretty typical. Okay.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

The only comment I am going to make is that, you know, the Hill Street connector in my neighborhood has been a game changer. So, and that was a long time coming, but it has made a difference. And again, the walkability that we are bringing with these small projects just adds to the quality of life in our neighborhoods and connecting them to our small area plans and our activity centers. Can't say enough of how these small projects are major game changers for neighborhoods where two of our council members live and possibly a third of our council member might live. So anyway, um, I will open the public hearing. Miss Shinaberry, has the public hearing been properly advertised? Yes, it has. I had one person sign up to speak prior to the meeting, so I will call that speaker up and then we'll open the floor if there's anybody else that would like to speak on this item. So our first speaker is Douglas Stewart.

turns.text: Thank you. Is there anybody else in the room that would like to talk on the Fern Street connector? Okay, um, so I will close the public hearing. There is no council action tonight on this issue. Okay, our next item is a public hearing and council action on an ordinance amending Chapter 102, in parentheses, Utilities, Article 2, in parentheses, Fees and Charges, Section 102-33 pertaining to sanitary sewer connection fees, frontage charges, and wastewater lateral repair and replacement charges, and Article For Wastewater Division 3, Connections to Public Sewers, Section 102-180 pertains to connections to public sewers. I'm going to recognize Annie Feary as a utilities coordinator. Yes, okay, yes, for the staff report. Good evening, Mayor and Council. My name is Anne Feary. I'm the utilities coordinator with the Department of Public Works. Um, this item proposes two changes to the utility ordinance. The first change would be to the Wastewater Lateral Repair and Replacement Program. City Council established the Lateral Repair and Replacement Program in January of 2017, along with a dedicated lateral repair charge to reimburse property owners for eligible sewer lateral repairs and replacements. The program also promotes trenchless rehabilitation methods to extend the life of aging laterals in a cost-effective manner. Since the program's adoption, inflation has signif— significantly increased material and labor costs. With input from the Finance Department, Public Works recommends increasing the maximum reimbursement amounts by 30%, from $2,500 for a partial replacement to $3,250, and then from $5,000 to $6,500 for full replacement. Expenditures, which both align with, um, cumulative inflation. These updates will help ensure the program continues to provide meaningful financial assistance and also support timely repairs. Um, the second amendment for this ordinance, uh, is a revision which clarifies how sewer availability charge credit is applied to existing structures. Um, the city is moving sewer availability fee processing and payment into the electronic ACELA portal, um, and we're coordinating with Code Administration, our department, Public Works, as well as the IT department. Um, currently determining credits for demolished structures requires extensive staff research and is often hindered by incomplete records. Um, the proposed amendments place responsibilities on applicants to identify prior structures and provide supporting documentation of that information. with their plumbing permit applications. That's typically when we receive availability and connection fees is when the trade permits are submitted. Um, we are also planning on noticing the applicants during the building and demolition process of this change that they will need to provide us the existing structure information. If documentation is not submitted, fees will be assessed without credit. Um, these changes will create a clearer and more consistent process for for the plumbing permit and building permit applicants. It improves the accuracy of sewer availability records and also supports the implementation of availability fee payments through the portal as a part of the city's broader goal of consolidating permitting and fee processing into Accela. That concludes the staff report for these two changes, and I'd be happy to answer any questions you might have. Okay, are there any questions of staff? Councilmember Amos.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Uh, thank you. Uh, I only had one question, kind of broken into two parts. So why the gap since 2017? Why wait for cumulative inflation instead of incrementally doing it? Or was that just missed?
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Thank you. This one is also a little bit of housekeeping. As you all are aware, the public may not be aware, but the, the, there's a statute in Virginia, State and Local Government Conflict of Interest Act, which establishes standards of conduct for certain officers and employees of state and local governments in the Commonwealth. The city has a corollary ordinance which goes beyond the simple requirements in the State and Local Government Act with respect to individuals or positions that are subject to annual financial disclosure requirements. And this ordinance would also update filing deadlines. There have been changes over the past few years, as well as continuing obligations, and also update the provisions related to violations and penalties. Just for clarification, previous to this ordinance, in addition to the enumerated positions, there was also a requirement, in addition to the department heads filing conflict of interest forms, that their deputies would file. In looking at this and comparing with what other jurisdictions do, we could not find any other jurisdiction jurisdictions that require that level of disclosure by— certainly by folks who are not in decision-making positions. So the recommendations are to adopt these changes. It also includes some updated title positions. We now have a Chief Financial Officer. We also include the commissioners of the Economic Development Authority and its executive director, as well as members of the Retirement Plan Administrative Committee. And then again, as noted, all department heads. It updates the requirements. The state requirements now are that these annual disclosures be filed by February 1st. There are also requirements for filing additional forms by certain enumerated individuals. This also reflects the state definition of what constitutes the immediate family of any of the enumerated positions, and that's defined as being any other person who resides in the same household as and is a dependent of persons subject to the scope of the division. The dependency requirement was a change at the state level. Reflecting that. And then we also updated the penalty provisions to correspond to the, uh, to the state enabling authority. With that, happy to answer any questions.

turns.text: Thank you, Mayor, Council. As discussed at the November 4th City Council Work Session and introduced at the December 9th City Council Regular Meeting, the City of Fairfax has previously been awarded $20.6 million in I-66 Outside the Beltway Project Concessionaire Funding for the George Schneider Trail project. The city recently received construction bids that exceeded the $16 million remaining in the project budget. The estimate to complete the project with additional costs such as contingency, construction management, and VDOT oversight will exceed the remaining budget by $4.6 million. There is additional concessionaire funding available in the balance entry account at VDOT to accommodate the city's funding need. Accordingly, the City requested this funding via the Northern Virginia Transportation Authority, NVTA, which is the entity that makes recommendations for this funding to the Commonwealth Transportation Board, also known as CTB.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: On November 13th, NVTA recommended approval of this funding to the CTB, and the CTB approved this funding request at their January 6th 2026 meeting. Happy to answer any questions. We have 35 speakers who've signed up before the meeting began tonight, and I have received 2 video testimonies that will be played after the in-person speakers. I will call the speakers who have signed up first. Videos, and then I will open the floor to anyone else in the room that would like to speak. So I will go ahead and call the first speaker up, which is Josh Silverman.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: I'm Josh Silverman, and my residence This is 4111 Sideburn Road in the Greenacre District. And I— with regards to the trail, from the point of view of the pedestrian or the biker, Fairfax City is separated from the remainder of the county. We do have a trail. Cross County Trail, which as the name suggests, is supposed to connect the communities of the county together, but it falls to pieces in the Fairfax City area. To the southeast of the city, there is a choice of two routes. The official route, as it currently stands, goes over Blake Lane and Germantown Road. It's all road walking. Uh, the— there is an alternative, the Wilcoxson Trail, which connects to the Cross County Trail, but that dead ends into Route 50. From there, it is well over a mile before you can connect to the underpass under I-66, and from there, um, to use the trail network to get across to Germantown Road. And from there, there is another trail that gets back to the Cross County Trail. And this is where the Snyder Trail comes in. The gap that we would currently have to fill by walking along Route 50 and Eaton Place, that is where the Snyder Trail would, uh, would take its place. And, uh, this route would divert pedestrians and bikers from highly trafficked and high-speed roads. And, uh, if, if it is, uh, this alternative is available, uh, people passing through the city, um, will be more likely to patronize the local businesses in the area that the proposed route, a trail, a Snyder Trail route, is supposed to pass, pass by. So I urge the council to take these factors into account: increased safety, increased revenue for businesses, and the just better impressions that the visitors will receive going through the City. Thank you.

turns.text: Our next speaker is Mike Fabio.

turns.text: Happy New Year. Mike Fabio, 3920 Chainbridge Road. First of all, congratulations on your $4.6 million award to complete the trail. Today, I'm sure you will hear testimony for and against the trail. I'm not here today to discuss in detail the many advantages of the trail. You've already heard this, that the trail will be available to people of all ages and abilities, that it will take people out of their cars and reduce air pollution, that it will connect neighborhoods and bring people together to establish a greater sense of community. That it will allow people to get out in nature and enjoy a safe environment, that it will provide overlooks and stations for educational opportunities, that it will replace more trees than it will take down, that it will be good for city businesses and tourism as it will draw people throughout the region to Fairfax City, that will enhance housing values. That trails are the one thing that young people want for their first home. They want to be near one. And that city residents have told you that they put trails high on their list of desires. I know you've heard all this before and you will hear it again, probably. In fact, at 10:15 PM on June 10th, 2025, after 12 years of planning, after reviewing the actions of previous city councils, after being briefed by numerous staff presentations, and after hearing hours of public testimony, you voted, and I quote, to proceed with the entirety of the project as designed and construct the George Snyder Trail. Tonight the ball is teed up for you to hit it out of the ballpark as you accept the additional funding. Take a victory lap as you capture this generación opportunity to add a major element to the city's infrastructure. $25.6 million without any tax burden to the public. Fantastic. In a few minutes, I hope we can all join in the celebration of this crowning achievement. Thank you.

turns.text: Our next speaker is Art Hammerschlag.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Good evening, Mayor and City Council. Art Hammerschlag, 384 43 Far Oak Circle. I am speaking tonight in favor of the George Snyder Trail. I will not repeat the many arguments you heard in favor. Instead, I would like to address the implications of refusing to appropriate additional funding provided to the city. We all recognize that this issue has divided city residents into two camps: those in favor and those opposed. Both camps have legitimate reasons for their position. The passions that have arisen over this issue are very strong indeed, as you can see from the number of people who are going to speak tonight. If the council appropriates these dollars, the matter is settled, over and done with. The trail will be built, no dollars need to be repaid, and the matter will fade into the past. Some folks will be unhappy, but the issue of the trail is over and done with. The residents can move on. But if the council fails to appropriate these dollars, what happens next? Bottom line, the council will have an ongoing series of unpleasant decisions to make, and the trail will continue to divide, inflame, and frustrate Fairfax City residents on both sides of the issue. The Council might try to downsize the project to build it within available funding, if you can get the votes to do that, but I don't think you can. But redesigning the project would require more engineering, more design, expenditure of more funds. And if the trail is not built, You will have more funds to pay back. Does the Council really want to continue to deal with this issue? Do you really want to go down this road, have the trail continue to be an ongoing, volatile, contentious issue with more votes, more public hearings, and paying back millions of dollars? I think it's in the best interest of the Fairfax City, its residents, and the Council to resolve this issue once and for all. End the squabbling, appropriate these dollars, build the George Snyder Trail, and then you, the Council, can look forward to dealing with more important issues the City has to deal with. Thank you.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Our next speaker is Janet Newhouse. Good evening. Janet Newhouse, 9706 Cardinal Road. I'm against additional funding for this trail. I have not spoken at a council meeting before, and I regret— deeply regret not doing so. And know there are many others who are against the trail who have not come forward. With 25 parks, 28 miles of trails in Fairfax City, we do not need this trail. Several nearby streets already provide neighborhood connections for bikers and for pedestrians. No studies have been done showing how many people might use this trail. How can it be built without knowing the potential users? Many presentations made by organizations and individuals concerned about the trail's environmental destruction seem to have been dismissed. There is an irony to Fairfax City's Urban Forest Plan when substantial damage will be done to trees, plants, and wildlife in an area which includes forest, floodplain, and a resource protection area. Yet hundreds of trees will be cut down, 6,900 feet of asphalt poured and a huge amount of soil removed in this vulnerable area when the city's 2024 resilience plan states it is anticipated the city will experience increases in rainfall, rainfall intensity, rainfall depths, and frequency and severity of extreme weather events. Water levels exceed the height of bank and they flood in Cambridge Station Park, range erosion Ranger Road Park, Akotink Trink, and there's a concentration in the Cambridge Station area, residents experiencing more frequent adverse flooding. I live directly across the street from the stream, have had years of flooding—yard, basement, etc.—extensive plumbing work, basement renovation, have spent almost $20,000 correcting this. Another The concern I have is the e-bikes and scooters on a paved path shared with pedestrians. Shared paths include collisions from speed disparities, crowding, inattention due to smartphones and headphones, with increased risk to slower pedestrians, seniors, and children. A recent report from the American Journal of Public Health says the rate of e-bike injuries almost tripled between between 2019-2022. With an expected increase of e-bike use of 10 to 16% in the next few years, will this number increase. I am already cautious when I walk because few bikers bother with signals, bells, horns, or verbal warnings, and it's dangerous walking out there. And I want to know who will be monitoring the use of this trail. Thank you very much. Okay, our next speaker is Tom Ross.

turns.text: Good evening, Mayor and Council. I reside at 3520 Country Hill Drive in the city of Fairfax and been a long-term resident for this place we call home, and one who has served in a variety of leadership roles, from civic engagement, boards and commissions, to sitting where you sit tonight as an elected official. I want to focus on the decision before you tonight, and especially for those of you who are uncomfortable voting to appropriate the additional money in the amount of $4.6 million concession funding for the George Schneider Trail project. You've heard from many people in the community over your service on council, as I did. I know there are people in the community that are strongly supportive, so— and many who are strongly opposed. To paraphrase Will Rogers, who once said, "Some of my friends are for it and some are agin it, and on this one I'm standing with my friends." That's the easy way out. The decision before you is whether to approve the supplemental appropriation in the amount of $4.6 million appropriation for the Snyder Trail to address the shortfall this construction and proceed with construction, or to reject the funding, ending a project supported by many in the community and region and placing the city in jeopardy of paying back $3.7 million. I want to repeat that. If you vote not to appropriate money tonight, you will be putting the city in jeopardy of paying back up to $3.7 million in funds that have already been expended, which works out to approximately 6 cents on our current tax rate. That would be a tremendous disservice to every citizen in this community, both present and future. Serving as an elected official requires making choices, often ones that you may feel personally uncomfortable with. I know I and countless other individuals who have served in elected positions have faced this. A number of you oppose the creation of the trail, and I get it. Tonight is different. You can vote tonight to not provide the necessary funding, funding, clearly understanding that you are putting the city and taxpayers in jeopardy. Furthermore, it will negatively reflect, impact our regional and statewide reputation as a partner who meets its obligations. Alternatively, you can be leaders who understand the impact of your decision and do what is right for current and future generations of this place we all love. I'm not going to describe to you the benefits of the completing the Snyder Trail. You've heard from me on numerous occasions and why I support this. Instead, I urge you with everything that I believe in to get this done. As elected official, there are many times when you have to weigh all the alternatives because it's the right thing to do. Tonight is one of those. Please do the right thing. Thank you.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Thank you. Our next speaker is Kenneth Bauer.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Good evening. My name is Kenneth Bauer. I reside at 8 Cleveland Court, Rockville, Maryland. I'm representing the Coalition to Stop Stream Destruction. We urge you also to be leaders and to do the right thing and to not fund the Stantec-designed George Snyder Trail. An unpaved trail through the woods already exists and a paved hiker/biker route along streets already exists, which could be improved and augmented by bike lanes along Fairfax Boulevard. Some of you may have known Armacista Tora. She was the first chair of Fairfax's Parks and Recreation Board. She was a family friend. I recently spoke with her son who said that she wanted trails. But would have been totally opposed to this kind of trail with its environmental destruction, the loss of hundreds of mature trees, countless smaller ones, shrubs, ferns, and wildflowers. And a previous speaker said, "Don't worry, they'll all be replanted." Well, yeah, they'll all be replanted with small saplings that will literally take 100 or more years to mature. In addition, the large amount of pavement this trail would create will send huge amounts of stormwater runoff into Accotink Creek, which I played along as a child. I grew up in Fairfax County. And this is going to cause a tremendous amount of erosion. I'm sure that the $4.5 billion Stantec Corporation that designed this trail would be happy to build on a multimillion-dollar stream restoration to fix the erosion that will be caused by their trail design. To see what that looks like, my written testimony has photos of Stantec's $2 million Dewey Creek stream restoration in Prince William County. These photos show how Stantec clear-cut the streamside forest. And if you want a local example of what that would look like, go to the Stafford Drive stream restoration here and look at the stream segment between Stafford Drive and Fairwoods Parkway. You can see the photo of this— or photos of this on the city's website, and you'll see a corridor that was cut through the forest, clear-cut, and a new stream channel was dug with exaggerated artificial sinuosity. In conclusion, we urge you not to fund the Stantec-designed George Snyder Trail since, as we— as I said, an unpaved trail through the woods already exists, a paved hiker/biker route along streets already exists which could be improved and augmented by bike lanes along Fairfax Boulevard. Thank you for your consideration.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Our next speaker is William Pitchford.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Good evening, and thank you for the opportunity to speak this evening. My name is William Pitchford. I live at 10609 School Street in the City of Fairfax. Later this evening, you will be asked by staff to approve a grant supplemental appropriation resolution from VDOT and to approve a contract award for the George Snyder Trail. I am asking you to approve both the grant in the contract award as it is the best option for the citizens of Fairfax City and for the city's overall economic and infrastructure development. I'd like to offer a few points as to why you should approve these two measures. The George Snyder Trail— the George Snyder Trail is an important part of the multimodal transportation infrastructure that connects Camp Washington, Northfax, and Fairfax City activity centers together. And it is spelled out in the city's smaller air plans. In the next few years, these three activity centers will account for the majority of Fairfax City's economic development. Having the right infrastructure will be critical for this growth. The two measures complete the trail in its entirety. In June of this year, this City Council, along with the citizens of Fairfax, voted to complete the full trail. The two measures fully fulfill the wishes of the voters. Not approving this grant would mean $21 million already obtained would be in jeopardy, and the city would have to repay another $3.7 million. and penalties with no trail to show for it. All total, that would be more than $29 million of economic activity lost by the citizens of Fairfax City with no trail to show for the years of planning and effort. The George Snyder Trail has broad support from the citizens of Fairfax City, city officials, cyclists groups and from the Fairfax City citizens for smarter growth. For these reasons, I'm asking you to accept the grant, approve the contract which will fully fund this trail system and completes it in the most expeditious manner. Thank you for your time.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Our next speaker is William Needringhaus.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Hello, my name is Bill Diedringhaus, President of Fairfax Trails and Streams. I organized the idea of the Fairfax Cross County Trail 27 years ago and organized several hikes across the county. As we entered the City of Fairfax, then Mayor Mason would greet our enthusiastic but weary hikers with very welcome refreshments. We hiked a route close to the George Snyder Trail as we envisioned a trail mostly away from traffic. With Jerry Conley's strong support from the top and our grassroots bottom-up effort, the trail officially opened in 2007. People love this 41-mile trail across the county with one glaring exception: the 3 miles beside fast traffic on Germantown Road and Blake Lane sidewalks. The trail bypasses the beautiful parks in the City of Fairfax, which could be its keystone. We work closely with the City of Fairfax for the last 15 years under various elected officials to help prioritize the long-planned wooded route for the Snyder Trail. We also work closely with VDOT's I-66 widening to make the trail along Route 123 as scenic as possible at our urging. VDOT improved the alignment. It's not immediately adjacent to Route 123. Instead, it uses the available open space in this huge intersection to make this route a pleasant future part of the Cross County Trail. It and the Snyder Trail together will fulfill the decades-long vision of a fully off-road Cross County Trail with the City of Fairfax at its heart. The Washington Area Bicycle Association Association got it right in their recent letter to the city. We have a fully funded infrastructure project supported by the city's own longstanding comprehensive plan that multiplies the connectivity and utility of existing local and regional trail networks to the benefit of the city residents and visitors alike, that was thoughtfully developed and revised by staff with plentiful opportunities for community input. The George Schneider Trail is clearly a project with enormous net benefits to the the city, its residents, and the larger region, and must be allowed to move forward. In our decades of following bike-related infrastructure, we cannot recall a jurisdiction backing out of a project this late in the project with so much dedicated funding at stake. To willingly hand back hard-won dollars at any time is eyebrow-raising, and to do so during a period of lean budgeting borders on malpractice. End quote. For the Snyder Trail, it's fourth and goal in overtime. Will you renege? Will you take a knee on fourth and goal in overtime? Will you break trust with VDOT, the CTB, and the State of Virginia? Please, no. Let's bring this trail home and support the Snyder Trail. Thank you so much.

I shudder to think of what unleashing those bulldozers on that, that wooded area will do to the environment, but also to the stream restoration project that we just completed. The runoff, the damage and the destruction of wetlands And yes, the destruction of several hundred more trees. Those of us who have lived next to this area in the stream for the last year or so, we've watched the destruction of all those trees for the stream restoration project. My name is Evangelia Efentidis, and I have been a resident in Fairfax City since 1993. I am here asking you to vote in favor of accepting the additional concessionaire funds and also to vote in favor of awarding the contract to construct the Snyder Trail to honor our past mayor and make a transformational legacy in all our future for decades to come. Bike commuters, runners, recreational cyclists, walkers, folks with strollers, kids, folks with mobility impairments using wheelchairs or walkers, park and nature enthusiasts, and so many others will gain so many added benefits from this trail. Well-constructed trails bring all kinds of humans outside, provide safe passage to school, work, fun, shopping, eating out, and giving— give us a sense of community. Isn't this what we always say about the great city of Fairfax? Many of you know that I commute— commuted 6.2 miles each way to work in Fairfax County Public Schools. I was able to ride a little over 10,000 miles in the 9 years I commuted to and from work. On my 1,000th ride, Mayor Meyer and Delegate Bulova came along. It was fantastic. We stopped at Daniels Run. It was great. The kids were wonderful. These 10,000 miles were made possible mostly because of safe and passable trails. We need more safe and passable trails that create these connections. During that same time frame, I also rode on Sunday mornings with a friend around Fairfax City. We did a 10-mile or a 12-mile loop. The Snyder Trail would make this loop much safer. Additionally, I was a member of the Parks and Rec Advisory Board from 2011 to 2021. This actually is one of the reasons I started biking to work. Bike enthusiast Frank Linton worked with a George Mason student to put together a bike path for students taking Metro de Viena and then biking to George Mason University. He came to PRAB to present this path in 2012. I asked him if he could find me a way to bike to work, and 2 days later, on a Saturday, he showed me 2 viable ways to commute— commuting options. Within the next 2 weeks, I started biking to work. You know, PRAB meets monthly. The Snyder Trail was added to the Comprehensive Plan in 2012. I remember Kathy Salgado, who was the director for most of my tenure, providing us updates during most, if not each of every of those meetings she ran. This trail has been talked about and discussed and worked on for thousands of hours in PRAB and other venues, including community forums, architectural and staff work. It is time to get the funds approved and get the trail built. Thank you for your time. Our next speaker is Leslie Cullen, then following is Janice Miller. Good evening, my name is Leslie Cullen and I live at 3411 Spring Lake Terrace. I'm here tonight again to express my continued support for fully funding the George Snyder Trail. And moving forward with awarding the contract. I believe the trail is an important investment in our community, supporting connectivity, safety, recreation, and long-term quality of life for residents. At the same time, I want to be candid in sharing that many of us in the community are growing weary of the repeated discussions, delays, and renegotiations surrounding this project and other developments. The George Snyder Trail has been studied, debated, revisited extensively. Continuing to reopen the same conversations creates uncertainty and slows progress on an initiative that has already broad support and a clear public benefit. I respectfully urge you to approve the funding and proceed with implementation so this project can finally move forward. Doing so would demonstrate a commitment to the thoughtful planning and decisive action, allowing our community to to focus its energy on the next set of priorities rather than revisiting the same ones again. Thank you. Next speaker is Janice Miller, then following is Judy Fisher. Good evening, I'm Janice Miller and a longtime city resident. Uh, Mayor Reed, council members, city staff, thank you for the opportunity to address the matter of George Snyder Trail once again. Putting this matter to bed is welcomed. It's time to make a final decision and to move on. That said, the follow-up question is simple. What's next? If council passes a supplemental appropriation resolution in the amount of $4.6 million for the George Snyder Trail and awards the contract, the trail moves forward. If the council votes 2-4 or 3-3 on the appropriation resolution, there will be inadequate funding to award the contract and the project is dead. Next question. What are the unintended consequences of a canceled George Snyder Trail? First, repayment of funds. There is an ABBA song that's "Money, money, money." As per city staff, $3.7 million will be needed to repay regional funding bodies. Question: Where are the funds available in the city budget? 2. Future outside funding. A council no vote or a 3-3 tie will send a message to regional funding bodies that the city council is not a dependable, reliable partner and that the newly— and that newly elected councils may not and will not in the future continue long-planned projects. Question: could grants for new city projects be at risk? Certainly, that's possible. Number 3, corporate and charitable giving. Recently, the City Council established a Parks Foundation board. It is my understanding the council also appointed members to begin the work of the foundation. One of the major tasks of this board will be to solicit funds from corporate— from corporations and charitable entities to receive recreational capital project funds as well as, as well as funds for recreational programs. Question: Will a no vote on tonight's appropriation motion affect the work of this group and hamper their efforts to solicit funds? Again, that is possible. Please remember, actions have consequences. City Council stability, reliability, and consistency agency go a long way in sending positive messages not only to city residents and businesses but to the broader community as well. City councils have forever completed plans previously developed by, by former councils. I would like to point out that last Saturday at the council's retreat, Councilmember McQuillan remarked that people need to remember that planned projects take 7 to 10 years. And at the same time, Councilmember Pedersen mentioned multiple times that the city needs to get a big bang for the buck. Trust me, George Snyder Trail has taken, it seems, forever, and it is a big bang for the buck. It's time to move forward. Please vote yes. MoveOn provides stability and reliability and consistency. Thank you for your time and attention. Next speaker is Judy Fisher, then following would be Tamara Harvey. My name is Judy Fisher, longtime resident of the city. I'm speaking tonight as part of the large majority of city residents who favor completion of the George Schneider Trail. I believe a small but very vocal group of people have left the current council with the mistake mistaken belief that a majority of residents would like to abandon longstanding plans to build the trail. Most of us thought this decision was finalized years ago. We are disappointed at the wasted time of the council, which has neglected needed business to discuss and discuss and then to renege on this plan. Do not think that delaying action again to allow the next council to act is a viable plan. Honor the decisions and financial commitments entered into by previous councils. We do not want to become known as undependable or for failing to honor commitments. Build the trail now. Our next speaker is Tamara Harvey, then following would be Jean Urrutia. Ricoli. Well done. Uh, good evening, I'm Tamara Harvey. I'm a resident of the city. I live at 10822 Maple Street. I commute to work by bike all year round, all weather, um, night, day. Um, I do errands by bike. I walk a great deal in the city. I see the George Snyder Trail as an important east-west route that helps connect the city to the county and commute— keep commuters like me safe. 50 is not a safe route, even on the sidewalk. We can't count on cheap and abundant fuel forever. We will have greater need of safe bike and pedestrian routes like this 10, 20 years from now, maybe sooner, maybe a lot sooner because of e-bikes and scooters. If we don't build this now with other people's money, we will build it in the future with our own. And with— and until then, people like me will continue putting ourselves at risk as we seek routes across the city. Please accept this funding and build the trail. Thank you. Next speaker is Jean Uricoli, and following would be Jane Kelsey.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Hi, good evening. I'm Gene Iricoli. I live in Mosby Woods. I've been here since 1986, myself and my family, obviously. And I recommend we do not take the money. Everybody's talking about it's a business decision. Everybody's talking about it's the right thing to do. I don't disagree with that. However, nobody's talking about the rape and the carnage of what's going on down there between Draper Park and 123. You're talking about going a beautiful piece of ground with the Akitik Creek runs through with its woodlands and everything else that's associated with the forest. I watched them take those trees out of that area during the creek restoration. All right, 250-year-old oaks, 4-foot diameter trunks, hundreds of them, 400 of them came out of there. The next piece of ground that gets worked on It's going to be 600-plus. I just walked the area twice over the past weekend. You could see it's going to be hard to do. Why would you tear up one of the most beautiful places in, uh, the city of Fairfax for a bike trail? You want to put a bike trail, put a bike trail where it can be accessed. You're going to tear up one of the jewels of Fairfax City for a walking trail and a bike trail. I don't oppose that. I think it's a great thing. Do the right thing in that regard. I've been here for 40 years. I've walked through that place with my 3 kids and my grandchildren. It's beautiful down there. You've got the creek, you got flora and fauna. I don't see how they're going to— when they tear that area up, the connect— try to connect the pieces of the Snyder Trail, it's, it's going to be mixed and matched. I'd be treasure— I'd want to see somebody ride a bike through there. You're going to have to go up up and down and round and round. The track— the ground is treacherous. So what I say, build the trail if you want to, don't appropriate the money now. Get a plan, rewrite the plan. If it's taken 15 years, fine, that means it's hard, it's hard work, you know, you want to get it right. Tearing up that piece of ground is not going to be the right thing to do. The segments I'm talking about, Draper Park to Stafford Drive, Stafford Drive to Fairwoods Parkway, which is right behind CVS. Go over there and look, it looks like somebody raped it with bulldozers and backhoes. And of course, Fairwoods Parkway to Route 123. Uh, let's see, one more piece before I'm done. You have a hard job, I respect that. There are existing walkways in this city, there are existing paths that already exist, they can be connected. Go through creatively, get somebody to do it, and connect them. Doing what? Oh, I'm sorry. All right, so in enclosure— yeah, finish the trail, but finish the right trail. Don't finish the one that takes one of the crown jewels of Fairfax City and tears it apart. It will not be a good decision. Go down to Cambridge Station and see what happened down there. They ruined that whole area. They ruined— thank you very much for your time.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Our next speaker is Jane Kelsey, then following is Mariana Fournier. Good evening. I'm Jane Kelsey, 4041 Autumn Court, Fairfax City. I've been in the City since 1961 and loved it, and I really I want to tell you that I support the trail. I think they are very important, and not only from my personal standpoint, but I've seen what it does for the other people in the community. We— it's for recreation, that's fine, but it is also for people who want to go up to Main Street. It's— it saves them getting in the car and driving a short distance. And if they've got a trail instead of having to go up and down the streets to get to Main Street, they wouldn't do it. They would take the car. So I think that helps many environmental issues that we have. So I just want to speak to that issue as well as the issue of saving taxpayer money, and I just do not see the math of how you could turn this down and when you've got $21 million and we have to pay $4 million. Just think about the difference between that. When you're just handed the $21 million, why would you give it back? And it seems to me you would just finish what you started a long time ago when it was in the plan, I believe, originally, and do it. It just seems so simple to me, but obviously I'm not having to make the decision for everybody in Fairfax City. But thank you very much. Next speaker is Mariana Fournier, then following would be Jeb Mann. Good evening, I'm Mariana Fournier, 10134 Spring Lake Terrace. The question we should be asking ourselves: if this was such a great project, why wasn't it done years ago? Why did it keep going forward and forward and costing us more and more money? Probably because of the way the council handled it. I'm sorry you weren't upfront with us. You weren't willing to listen to us. We gave you a petition and it was sneered at. If you think we're going to trust the city council with spending more money You gotta be kidding me. I saw the picture today of what you've already done. This isn't— this is environmental rape. I'm sorry, but if this was a good program, then you should have worked it out some way and have gotten it taken care of years ago. I believe it was because you dug your heels in and there was only one way to do it and you weren't willing to compromise. You weren't willing to listen to us. And you— and the prior councils just frankly blew it. Why should we trust you anymore? And as far as spending money, this is a money pit. It's going to cost more and more and more because you haven't really done this appropriately. There are going to be additional costs that you are not even thinking about. And we're saying not a penny more. And if anyone wants to discuss the issue with me, you know where I live, and I'll even make the coffee. But I wish you had this prior councils had been willing to sit down and talk to their citizens instead of assuming that they had the right answer. I mean you no disrespect. Consider this a fraternal correction. Thank you very much. Next speaker is Jeff Mann, then following would be Henry Mann.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: My name is Jeff Mann. I am at 9744 Cardinal Road in the great city of Fairfax. I have heard many eloquent people tonight on both sides of the issue. But I, I encourage you to vote no. Um, this— we need safety and we need walkways and we need, uh, things like that in this city, but we do not need the George Schneider Trail as proposed because it's bloated. The, uh, environmental study that was done on it is flawed. There are protected species and wetlands out there that are going to be decimated. It's It's the last, one of the last stands in the city of hardwood forest, and it's beautiful like the gentleman spoke about before. I've walked around it with my family, and it's wonderful. And the animals we haven't even talked about, like the blue heron that's in there, the one that I've seen. There might be more. It's always there. The ducks, the fox, the raccoons, all of that is going to be messed up. Mr. Ito, word on the street is, has decided not to plant some of the trees in the stream restoration because it's going to mess up what's proposed for the George Schneider Trail. So that's even less trees that are going to go up because the Schneider Trail is going to decimate more trees. Um, it's a tough decision, and, and you guys work hard, I know that, but you've got to think of your fiduciary responsibility to the citizens and the future of Fairfax City and my son and the people who come in and move in here years and years and years from now. Don't get rid of those woods. Don't mess up those woods. It's the only thing we've got that really lets us know that we're a tree city. I mean, we speak out of both sides of our mouths, and it's kind of crazy. It's time to stop the madness.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Just vote no. Our next speaker is Henry Mann, then following is Kathleen Brown.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: My name is Henry Mann. I live at 9744 Cardinal Road, and that was my dad that just spoke, and I wanted to correct correct him, there are actually two herons that we have seen together. They're— I mean, I can confidently say I've lived in the same house all my life. I can confidently say that I know the woods that the George Schneider Trail is going to go through more than basically anybody in this room. I know when I think of the George Schneider Trail and like the area that it's going to go through, I think not necessarily of the woods as much as the people that go through it. I think that there's a lot of waste that I've had to pick up in the last week. I walk to school. Going home, I've picked up at least 10 pounds of trash in the last 3 days. Wait, it's a Tuesday. 2 days, sorry. That's like, that's where my mind's at right now, but if more people come in there, that's going to be more more of an issue. We're going to have to worry about cleaning up trash. We're gonna have to worry about— I mean, I've heard about policeman numbers too, right? We have to add more policemen to the staff, and the police is understaffed as it is. I might be wrong on that. Anyway, I wanted to clarify some more things that people have said though. The idea that the George Snyder Trail really crosses through the city and crosses and passes the city's businesses. It's Wawa to Best Western, and there's woods, and there's nothing else. If we really want to advertise Fairfax City as it is to the greater people, which we are very considerate of, to the greater county, we should pass it through the real part of Fairfax City where the businesses are, where we want to showcase what we have.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Also, we have plenty of great projects.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Think about the Fern Street stuff that we talked about at the beginning. We have a lot of great small connector projects that if we worked on those, connecting communities together, we would have a much— I mean, we would have a much stronger feeling of a unified city. We shouldn't have a line cutting off Mosby Woods from everything else. Thank you.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Our next speaker is Kathleen Brown, then following is Chris Finch— French. Good evening, I'm Kathleen Brown. I live at 3230 31 Juniper Lane in Falls Church. In the words of sage David Attenborough, ancient trees are precious. There is little else on Earth that plays host to such a rich community of life within a single organism. I speak tonight as a board member of Climate Action Now, an organization which educates through the presentation of environmental films and documentaries at cinema, arts, theater, right here in Fairfax City on the very urgent issues of our time: extreme weather, record temperatures, diversity loss, ecosystem stress, and among others. After these films, panel discussions with local experts and attendees from all over Northern Virginia are a testament to your very— residents' growing engagement on urgent climate matters. I also speak as a member of a group of residents in the Seven Corners area of Fairfax County, which successfully fought off paving of 2 acres of land in our area of the county with the highest socioeconomic needs and the lowest amount of parkland. Half of Justice Park's 18 acres is an urban forest with a tributary of Tripps Run, which runs into Lake Barcroft nearby. Justice Park's 8-acre forest is a smaller version of yours. Having walked portions of your trail, the proposed extensions clearly struck me that while our 2-acre paving inside Justice Park was a parking lot, this planned bike trail is an urban forest superhighway. It cuts deep, wide, and through the very center of the forest. Your costs in tree loss stress on Akatin Creek, loss of plant and animal habitat, and decades of soil enrichment are too high a price to pay. While improved pathways for bike and pedestrian travel are certainly valuable, the proposed route for the extension and its massive retaining walls are too intrusive, destructive, and costly in funding tree and animal habitat loss, watershed stress, and soil degradation. Preservation over paving is the best path to take through your remarkable and rich urban forest. Your determination this very evening will be felt for generations of human, animal, tree, and plant life, not only for our common survival, but for our flourishing. Thank you for this opportunity to weigh in. Next speaker is Chris French. Following is Philip Latasa.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Thank you. Good evening, Mayor and Council. My name is Chris French. I live at 3172 Stratford Court, and I'm the president of Northern Virginia Families for Safe Streets, an all-volunteer organization focused on advocating for street safety, specifically pedestrian safety, throughout the region. We have more than 2,000 members and have chapters in Alexandria, Arlington, and Fairfax. I do communicate regularly with Chloe Ritter regarding the city's transportation initiatives, and we were happy to be offered a seat at the table in the Move Fairfax City Advisory Committee. I've come today to speak in strong support of the George Snyder Trail. I'll start by sharing that our Fairfax chapter is a member of Fairfax Healthy Communities, a network of 22 nonprofits working in the intersecting areas of active transportation, Smart Growth, Environment, Housing, and Food Security. Network partners envision a region where people can live, work, and play in connected communities that are healthy, sustainable, and inclusive. We believe safe and comfortable routes to walk, bike, use transit, and get to the places we need to and want to go without relying on driving is integral to making our communities more sustainable and healthier. And contributes to a local— a vibrant local economy. This project works to achieve that vision. I certainly acknowledge the trail's construction will not be without impact. However, I believe there are considerable benefits that will be realized. The trail will provide a much-needed, currently missing connection between the 66 Parallel Trail and the CCT and Wilcoxon Trails, in addition to providing connectivity to this larger regional trail network that The trail will provide benefits to local residents by expanding active transportation options for all ages and all abilities to get to school, work, Metro and other transit, restaurants and retail, and many other destinations comfortably, safely, and with low levels of stress. Active transportation projects such as these reduce car dependency and VMT, or vehicle miles traveled, thereby aligning with regional climate goals due to the accompanying reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, but they also help in other ways, such as reducing acute tailpipe pollutants, mitigating traffic congestion and suburban sprawl, while encouraging physical activity, social connection, and overall improvements to public health. Further, greater access to the outdoors for all ages and abilities can help grow appreciation for and stewardship for our natural resources. To close, I'll say that I often find myself at hearings meetings such as this, speaking in support of policies and actions which put people's needs at the center of decision-making to ensure safe, livable, accessible, sustainable, and green communities. Thank you for allowing me the time to do that— just that again tonight.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Next speaker is Phil Watasa, then following is John Russell.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: I speak tonight representing the Friends of Accotink Creek regarding the George Snyder Trail. Down, down, down into the darkness of the grave. Gently they go, the beautiful, the tender, the kind. The fish, the toad, the fern, the sycamore, the maple. Down they go for our convenience, for our whims, for our sins. Down they go that we adults may ride our toys upon their two dooms. The year is 2026. It is not 1726 when colonists carved a new society toward the horizon. It is not 1826 when land without end was needed to feed and clothe our growing multitudes. It is not 1926 when the newly ascended automobile could spread its tracks over an unpaved continent. No, the year is 2026 when we find ourselves become the guardians of our last remnants of woods and wetlands. when the insatiable demands of humans have backed the natural world to the brink. When it is time to curb our desires in favor of protecting what remains of nature. We have learned now that the cost of the George Schneider Trail has become too high in dollars we can count. We should have learned before the start that the cost was already too high in those things whose value is beyond counting. Please do not continue with the George Schneider Trail or similar projects to pave through our last woods and wetlands. Thank you.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Next speaker is John Russell. Then following is Douglas Cox.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Madam Mayor, members of the City Council, my name is Cappy Russell. I live in the Daniels Run Woods neighborhood. The clerk has my exact address. My friend Brian has a useful taxonomy for understanding intractable issues like this. It's the difference between good reasons and real reasons. I've spoken to this body before and corresponded with many of you about the good reasons to build the George Snyder Trail: creating an amenity accessible for all of the city's residents, diversifying our transportation infrastructure so that all residents Residents can choose how best to move about and beyond their homes into the wider world and back again. Integrating our community with neighboring towns and maybe introducing ourselves to visitors from further afield, attracting new customers for our local businesses, new residents for our city. Being true to our word and a reliable partner in the eyes of development organizations and government agencies we might want to call upon in the future. And then of course there's the matter of minding the city's finances carefully and not incurring a needless penalty, a penalty of more than $3 million. No small matter, that. From where I stand, this council has all the good reasons it needs to approve construction of the trail and help the city take this next logical step in its development. That we are still here debating this measure again so many months later suggests to me that all the good reasons in the world are not reaching the real reasons for debate and delay. My fear is that the real reason some on the Council and some in the city say no is a certain allergy to any development, any growth, any change in our city. If that is what you on the Council are feeling or responding responding to, if the real reason to oppose the trail is the desire to encase the city in amber and reject development in all of its forms, a trail today, an apartment building, a health center, let me suggest that that is not what it means to be a city. Cities are living, breathing things. They grow and change and develop. And that dynamism is the point. It's what many people choose to live in a city for. It's the source of a city's prosperity. To curb it or to stall it is to risk the long-term health of your city. Cities that stop changing start withering. So please vote tonight with optimism about Fairfax City's future life. Please vote tonight to fully fund and to build the George Snyder Trail. Thank you for your time.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Okay, next speaker is Douglas Cox, and following is Avril Garland.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Good evening, Douglas Cox, Fairview Drive. You guys have my address. Anyways, uh, most of you have already made up your minds. I'm not for it. I've actually— unlike 99% of the city ridden all the way around to get to the 66 Trail from Fairview. Long way. Uh, it's accessible, it's easy, it's not a big deal, it's entirely safe. So not a fan of this. Plus, let's be honest, you don't have metrics to say how many people use the I-66 Trail at 123. It's built, it's there. How many people cross? How many use it? Unique users, individual users? You don't know. No one ever took metrics. Never figured out how much sediment goes into the stream if you build this thing. You know, you spend all that money on the TMDL and now you're gonna blow it by wiping away and putting this trail. So anyways, let's get into some truth because it seems like a lot of people are confused here. This trail does not come anywhere near the cross-county trail to the west. Cross County Trail goes up Blake Lane, Germantown, ends up going behind Oakmont. So people going, you go through the city and you meet the cross country— not even close. If Oakmont and the Moose Lodge are next to each other, that's fantasy world. Let's stop with that nonsense. And on the other side, let's be truthful, city scene, uh, what do we say here? ADA accessible trail complete. An important missing link between I-66 trail through the city to connect with the Jerry Connelly Cross Country Trail. It does not. George Snyder Trail does not connect to the cross country trail. Will Coxon Trail does. So if you come down the Snyder Trail to the east, go down 50 a little ways, the unsafe highway that we can't get on, catch Will Coxon, then you hit the CCT. But saying they connect is like saying Washington State connects to Alaska. All right? They don't. So let's quit with the nonsense, quit with the lies. Let's all be honest about what this does, where it goes, and also how did we get here. This thing almost got killed in the May-June timeframe. And the city was supposed to have until December. To talk amongst ourselves. And then all of a sudden VDOT threw out, nope, you— in May, you have to decide by June. So talks were made, votes were done. June, you guys sent— June 10th, you sent a thing out to VDOT and said, yep, we're going forward. The bill came in, it was hired. Now here we are talking about it. And I got to say, this is a bad look. Bill Cutler, who wrote the letter from VDOT that said make your decision by June, 2 days after you made the decision, here's the mayor with Bill Cutler, thumbs up. It's a bad look. If there was collusion, shame on you.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos
