You have a terrible job ahead of you. I thank very much the 3 council members who have had the foresight and courage to oppose this wrong-headed recreational development, which is undesired by many residents. Residents. Honestly, I can't tell. Somebody said the opposing side, they have broad— said they have broad support. I'm against it. I see people here against it. But how many people are against the trail? How many people want the trail? Do we even know this? I know Arlington County did a survey on deer. Do we want to cull the deer? Sharpshooting, archery, private yards, parks. I mean, I don't think we know what residents I suggest you go and find out, go door to door. The other thing we're talking— the other issue that you talked about was the gain. Yes, you do have a certain amount of gain. I'm on a bicycle and I don't particularly like using Route 50 or Route 66. I mean a path through the— a paved path through the woods. Yeah, I'd like that. But look at the cost. I don't want that to come with the price tag. Price. These barred owls live in the woods, the herons, the finches, the thrushes, the deer, the salamanders, the box turtles. These woods are— to feed them, to feed 500 trees to the chipper, that's a terribly high cost. Or to replace the 500, 600 trees by little sticks and tubes that it's going to take 100 years to grow, as somebody pointed out. Yeah, the trail would be nice, but the cost is really so terrible. You'd be losing— think of what you would be losing. And I think what you're losing would be greatly more than what you're gaining. So I'd ask you to please oppose the trail or find some other ways of making the roads safer. Thank you. Our next Speaker:ELLEN PENNS, 3211 ADAMS COURT. I just realized I have been a resident of Cambridge Station for 58 years now. I am going to read a letter that was written to you today to was given to me. It says, dear council members, I have asked Ellen Pence to read this letter on behalf of me, Rebecca Caldwell, president of Mosby Woods Condominium Association. I speak on behalf of the 168 families who live in our community. We were extremely disappointed to learn in the last 2 years that our entire forest forest would be removed with a creek restoration project and a bike roadway. A dozen years ago, we lost one-third of our neighborhood forest but gained a field and playground in Stafford Park. Right now, we cannot stand to have any more large projects. We watched our mature forest be taken away by stream restoration. Initially, we were very excited about the prospect of lowering the floodplain. However, the new creek has already flooded our homes. If the other half of our forest is removed, the runoff problem will be worse. There are also ponds of standing water, which will be horrible in about 2 months, as soon as the weather is warm enough for mosquitoes. We are told that they were a stake and are going to have drainage ditches done, but there is still standing water. Who is going to maintain this properly? Our forest is already half gone. Please don't take the other half. The children of our neighborhood used to explore and look for wildlife in and around the creek and play in the woods. We liked the fresh air filtered by the mature forest. We had a barrier between us and Stafford Park and Fairfax Boulevard with all of the trees. We are now having problems with rodents and have seen foxes, raptors, raccoons, and deer come— coming in close to the buildings since there is nowhere else for them to live. The surrounding neighborhoods are fine with bike lanes going on Cardinal Road. Please use this route instead taking down the rest of our mature forest. The wetlands to the west of us are protected so that that part of the project cannot go forward. Please leave things the way they are. My final thoughts for you are these: our community does not want to be disturbed with any more construction, and we want to keep what small part of our mature forest remains. Thank you. Rebecca Caldwell. President Mosby Woods Council of Co-Owners. Next speaker, Sophie McNeil. Then following is Toby Sorensen. Hello, my name is Sophie McNeil. I live at 3417 Newberry Road, and for my whole life I've lived in Country Club Hills and grew up playing in the forest. You know, I'm speaking for myself, family, friends, neighbors who cannot be here tonight but are also opposed to the trail. Not only are there numerous environmental issues with funding this trail, but now it's come to light that this trail is incredibly over budget, and for such a short length of trail. As mentioned many times, a trail system already exists, and I'd like to ask again, how well are those maintained? How can we trust that the new trail will be taken care of? Um, I could recite many environmental issues that this trail poses, but you've heard them all before. And I just graduated with a bachelor's, my degree in environmental science. I've worked on wetland restorations, forest surveys, all that type of stuff. And the trail would just destroy vital ecosystems, and they just won't bounce back by planting saplings. Um, passing these plans, supporting the budget addition. Though that's not the right decision, it is the selfish one. Can we think about something other than our own personal benefit, these anthropocentric views, for once? I urge you guys to please stop these destructive plans. And not to mention, I've heard we may not even have to repay it. Thank you very much. Our next speaker is Toby Sorensen, and following is Jennifer Rose.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Good evening, everyone.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: During the time I was a city school board member, I served as the board's representative to the Environmental Sustainability Committee for 3 years. I also served as the board's representative to the Parks and Recreation Advisory Board for 5 years. This was during the recession of 2008 when recreational funds were severely restricted. We spent a lot of time discussing how to deal with limited spending. Under the direction of the forward-thinking director Mike McCarty, and later by— with his successor Kathy Salgado, we worked with staff to develop a comprehensive plan that would set priorities and outline how to accomplish them. One of these priorities was the building of the George Snyder Trail as part of the cross-county connector. The county was considering bypassing the city, which is at the midpoint of the county. That made no sense to us at all, so we advocated to have the connector pass through the city, to have a small part of the trail give accessibility to the elderly, to people with mobility issues, and to parents with small children is a good thing. To have that part of the trail within the city is a good thing. Walking and cycling without having to breathe in automobile fumes is a good thing. This is an issue about trust as much as it is about trails and dollars. Taxpayers trust you to be good stewards of our money. Residents who need an accessible path through the woods are counting on you. Planning projects, especially joint projects, take a long time, longer than any one 2-year term in office. The state and county had faith in our elected leaders that they would think beyond a single election cycle. So did all of us who work toward the goal of having the Cross County Connector Trail pass through our community. Trees can be replanted, habitats can be restored. It is trust that is hard to rebuild. Please maintain our trust in you and vote to fund the full George Snyder Trail. Thank you for listening. Next speaker is Jennifer Rose, and following is Shelley Vance. [FOREIGN LANGUAGE] Good evening, Mayor, members of Council. I'm here tonight to express the Central Fairfax Chamber of Commerce's strong support for appropriating the additional $4.6 million in Transform 66 Outside the Beltway concession funding and to award the contract for the George Snyder Trail. We recognize that this project has generated significant discussion as we we have seen this evening and many, many weeks in the past within the community. I'm often asked, why does the Chamber have a position on the trail? Some of you have asked me this question. The answer is quite straightforward. This decision is fundamentally about economic development, fiscal responsibility, and the city's long-term competitiveness. From the Chamber's perspective, the choice before you is clear. First, if this project is not completed, the city could be required to repay nearly $4 million to VDOT. That repayment would come from the— directly from the general fund and ultimately be borne by city residents and business owners. In contrast, the trail's completion does not require general fund dollars. It's a grant from VDOT. As council enters a challenging budget season, just like last season's, there's going to be difficult trade-offs and potential tax increases under consideration. The city can ill afford to spend millions of taxpayer dollars repaying funds for a project that's not going to be built. Second, while the George Snyder Trail may not run directly past every business— and I want to put Henry Mann in an internship, he's concerned about business— its value to the business community is undeniable. Businesses succeed within strong citywide systems, not in isolation. Trails improve connectivity between neighborhoods, employment centers, and destinations, expand transportation options for employees and customers, and enhance overall quality of life, an increasingly important factor in business retention and expansion, as well as talent recruitment. Employers and workers alike are drawn to communities communities that invest in accessible, forward-thinking infrastructure. Importantly, this project represents a long-term return on investment. Economic development is not measured solely by immediate revenue but by sustained growth over time through increased property values, stronger business attraction and retention, increased visitation that supports local businesses, and a city brand that reflects thoughtful strategic leadership, the George Snyder Trail contributes meaningfully to these long-term outcomes. Finally, there's the matter of reputation and future funding. This trail has been extensively planned, publicly vetted, and supported with external funding. Returning those funds or scaling back the project would send the wrong signal about the city's reliability as a partner in regional infrastructure efforts. It could jeopardize future state, regional, and federal investments and squander years of planning, coordination, and community engagement. For these reasons, the Central Fairfax Chamber of Commerce respectfully urges Council to appropriate those funds and approve the contract. This will allow the project to move forward. We appreciate your consideration and your continued commitment to the economic health and future of the City of Fairfax. And always, we are grateful for your leadership and service to this— Thank you. Next speaker, Shelley Vance, and following is Lauren Hillman. Hi, I'm Shelley Vance. I live at 3220 Cambridge State— Cambridge Court. The George Snyder Trail design through the woods with the loss of some 600 trees makes a mockery of our reputation as a tree city and our urban forest management plan. It is an unnecessary destruction of the environment and likely threatens the effectiveness of the recently completed Stafford area stream restoration. Do not build this trail through our woods, woods and wetlands. We are not opposed to a trail and bike path that connects paths that lead to and away from the city, but there are other better, less damaging routes for the placement of this trail. Thank you. Next speaker is Lauren Hillman, then following is Jessica Locke. Madam Mayor, City Council members, my name is Lauren Hillman. I live at 3307 Preserve Oaks Court, one of many locations I've lived in the city for the past 18 years. This trail was first conceived almost 20 years ago and was included in the city comprehensive plan in April 2012. We have had 7 city council elections since then. This means the 5 previous councils have not felt the need to rehash this. It's only come up in the past 3 years, and frankly, in those 3 years, have we had a single city council meeting when we don't discuss this trail? It already takes so long to get things done in the city. I'm still waiting on the Blenheim Boulevard rehabilitation project to get started, and I attended meetings about that over 10 years ago. The trail— this trail has been in the works almost 20 years. Why are we still talking about it? Why can we not get it done? Fairfax City is a wonderful place to live, but the red tape and bureaucracy have got the city at a standstill. We need to move forward. We need to make progress. We need to stop vacillating over this trail. Too many city residents reject out of hand any proposal to move the city forward. Or to support people other than themselves. They demand that the city remain stagnant, the same as it ever was. That is neither realistic nor sustainable and is not— it is not what Fairfax City is about. Fairfax City should be a place of inclusion where we plan for growth responsibly, welcome change thoughtfully, and ensure that progress benefits all members of our community, not those— not just those who are already comfortable or those who have a voice. Residents complain about taxes, but they don't want affordable housing or a modernized health center and community center to bring more people into the city to help contribute to those taxes. They complain about the unhoused population while blocking the very projects that would give people in crisis training and places to live. They complain about traffic but don't want to build trails to make our community more accessible, walkable, and bike-friendly. Please, let's stop talking and just get this trail built. Every vote the council takes delay and rework this project, the cost of this trail continues to rise. Real dollars drained from our budget as construction costs increase. Real damage to our city's reputation because we can't seem to get past this one issue that was decided 20 years ago. And real harm to the people who live here by continuing to focus on this one project and taking time and money away from other concerns. I trust that you will make the right decision, accept the grant, and keep our city from becoming frankly, an embarrassment. Thank you. Next speaker is Jessica Locke, then following is Ann Hammond. Hello, good evening everybody. I live at 10505 Cobbs Grove Lane and my name is Jessica Locke. I'm a cyclist, I'm a runner, and I'm a climate and environmental expert. but today I'm speaking as a Fairfax City resident opposing the George Snyder Trail. I presented to this Council— not this Council, the Council— twice before, and my professional expertise was dismissed by some members in favor of personal opinion not supported by facts. So please let me be clear about my credentials tonight. I design corporate climate and environmental strategies, assess financial climate risks, and advise on sustainable construction for large global companies like Meta, Amazon, and GE. These companies trust my expertise, and they trust my ability to help them make financially sound decisions because I'm pragmatic and because I don't believe in preservation for the sake of preservation. So please allow me to share some facts with you tonight. Trees clean our air. They filter pollutants like dust sulfur dioxide and reduce downwind pollution from high-traffic areas like the I-66 corridor by 30%. 30% cleaner air. Trees reduce noise. That is an established fact. Research shows that trees reduce noise pollution by anywhere from 5 to 10 decibels and reduces perceived noise in surrounding communities by up to 50%. Trees contribute to better health. Research conducted by the EPA links trees and green spaces to reduced heart rate, blood pressure, and stress and anxiety. And finally, trees save money. 100 trees absorb 250,000 gallons of rainwater. That's the equivalent of the Oakmont Rec Center pool during every single heavy rainwater event. That in turn reduces loads on infrastructure, lowers flooding risk of surrounding communities, and increases motorist safety. I want to talk a little bit more about this particular metric because with the destruction of the trees for the George Schneider Trail, 1.5 million gallons of rainwater runoff will be handled through gray infrastructure or the nearby creek that is currently mitigated by, uh, for free by the existing trees. I can guarantee you with that metric alone that maintenance and damage repair costs will quickly and far exceed any amount that has been stated tonight. I believe $3.7 million is the figure I've heard, uh, repeatedly the city would have to pay back. Enclosing the George Schneider Trail is unnecessary, it's harmful, and it is expensive. This, combined with other proposed or in-progress projects sets a dangerous and expensive precedent, and I urge you, please do not buy into the sunk fallacy cost— the sunk cost fallacy that nearly everybody here tonight in favor of the George Schneider Trail has presented to you today. Please follow facts and data. Thank you. Our next speaker is Ann Hallman, and then following her would be Tom Blackburn. Good evening, Madam Mayor and City Council. My name is Ann Hammond. I live at 3226 Dunster Court, Cambridge Station. I have watched the trees be devastated there along parallel to Cardinal Road and stream restoration. Now the proposal is to remove some more trees for the George Schneider Trail, just adjacent to the trees that have been removed. I've heard many good arguments for and against tonight. Sometimes I almost got swayed to the foresight, but then reason took over and I remembered what I have to look at when the trees are gone. We cannot lose more trees. I don't want to lose any more trees. The little twigs and shrubs that they've planted now, they're going to take years. As you can tell by my gray hair, I don't have years for those little twigs and shrubs to grow into old growth. We took away a lot of old growth. I think that the lady who bragged about doing 10,000 miles of bike ride Get in touch with her. Find out where she rode. Maybe you can make some connections with some trails that she took for safety. And as far as business growth, unless you're in Vietnam and you look out the bus window and you see the scooters with all manner of things that they're transporting, I don't think I'm going to see a 2x4 from Home Depot going down the road on a bike. I don't see— I think I see a big load of groceries for a family of 4 on a bike going down the road. You can connect communities, but I don't necessarily buy that this is going to just go and flourish with all kinds of business growth. That's not the only reason. And the future maintenance of this trail is going to come out of my pocket, out of the other pockets of all the people that are in favor of it, because maintenance is not going to be covered by what you want to accept tonight for this trail. So thank you for listening to me. I know you've heard pros and cons. Some of you have made up your minds, but goes either way, and I wish you good luck. Thank you. Our next speaker is Tom Blackburn. You can go ahead and speak.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Well, I can't because they need to see that. Okay. Really tried to get this in for a video conference taped, uh, uh, speech, but didn't— missed— I missed a deadline, so Thank you. Good evening. I'm Tom Blackburn of the Northern Virginia Bird Alliance. I want to give you a visual sense of the extent of the destruction at just a few of the points on the George Snyder Trail using photos taken at the locations shown on Stantec's design plans for the City's website. Slide 1 is a view from the start of the trail at Route 123. The first excavation starts just 150 feet away. Two-thirds of this trail is going to be significantly above or below the existing grade. Slide 2. At 280 feet from the start, the trail will be 8 feet below grade. On the right side, the slope to connect with the surrounding terrain will be 22 feet wide And on the left side, there will be a retaining wall 10 feet high plus footers 3 feet below grade. Slide 3 gives you an idea of how far below grade the excavation will be at that point. And the excavation is 275 feet long. Slide 4. The trail switches from above ground to below ground at 425 feet from the start. Here at 500 feet on the slide, the trail will be built on 4 feet of fill dirt with slopes 32 feet wide to grade down to the existing grade. The trail will be on fill for 175 feet. Slide 5. A new section of trail begins on fill dirt 775 feet from the start. At 900 feet here, the fill dirt will be 3 feet high with slopes to the surrounding terrain 23 feet wide. This section of fill is 255 feet long, and then the trail switches back to below ground again. Slide 6. At 2,000— 2,075 feet from the start, the trail will be built on 8 feet of fill dirt to reach the first of 4 14-foot-wide bridges. The slope to the surrounding terrain will be 40 feet wide. Slide 7 shows you just how far above grade the trail will be at the point where it meets the bridge. Slide 8. West of Fairwoods Parkway, the trail will be built on the side of a steep slope requiring retaining walls. 675 feet west of Fairwoods, the retaining wall will be 14 feet high plus footers 5.5 feet below grade. The wall will be 312 feet long. Slide 9. East of Fairwoods, there is significant cross slope requiring a retaining wall 3 to 6 feet high and 175 feet long. To make the trail level from side to side. Slide 10, 75 feet east of Fair— Stafford Drive, the trail will be up to 6 feet below grade, requiring excavated slopes 36 feet wide to meet the surrounding terrain. That excavation will be 150 feet long. Slide 11 gives you an idea of how far below grade the trail will be at that point. If you vote to fund this trail, your legacy will be the destruction of much of Fairfax City's urban forest. Thank you.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Mr. Blackburn was the last speaker who signed up prior to the meeting. I'm going to go ahead and do the two video testimonies that we received. Give me one second. All right, our first video is from Marissa Perrone. Hi, my name is Marissa Perrone. I live at 9892 Cardinal Road. I'm speaking tonight in support of the George Schneider Trail. I have two small kids. That's why I can't be with you tonight. But they are big fans of walking and biking everywhere. They think it's so much more fun than riding in a car, and I do too. I think good trails build good neighbors. And I think that if we were more connected to places that are really within walking distance, we'd see a lot more people visiting city businesses and, you know, driving local traffic to those businesses. You look at places like Arlington, and that's just what we're lacking here in Fairfax is is that connectivity and feeling that togetherness that trails bring. I wanna share a story of something that happened recently. I was visiting Compass Coffee with my daughter. She was riding along in a bike trailer with me. So very obviously a mom and her 7-year-old daughter behind her. And we visited Compass Coffee on the way back. We pushed the button to get the pedestrian sign to cross the road at Eaton Place, and, um, someone waited for us, uh, someone who is taking a right turn waited for us to, um, cross the street, waited very patiently. Well, a line of 5 cars got behind this person and they were laying on their horns, just absolutely raging at the fact that someone waited, uh, to cross. Um, and it's because we're not used to seeing pedestrians in this area. Um, it, it, and bikers, it feels like we don't even exist. Um, everyone's just so focused on getting to where they need to be in a car-centric society that they're not really thinking about people on bikes. So I was very grateful that somebody was willing to wait for me, but a little shocked that so many people just thought it was ridiculous that someone would wait for me even though it's the law. I think building more, you know, bike-centric spaces would create more of a sense of safety for all of us. And, you know, for our kids too. I think that I would love to see a world where our kids are able to like walk to a coffee shop to hang out with their friends. That would promote a sense of independence that I think that our generations are really lacking. So I think I think that supporting this trail is supporting the future of Fairfax, and let's think about our kids when we vote. Thanks. Our next video is from Sherry Steely. Good evening. I'm Sherry Steely, and I'm here tonight to talk about the George Snyder Trail project. The project intends to transform this small footpath through the woods adjacent to Ranger Road Park into a 14 to 16-foot wide paved road, necessitating the removal of more than 500 mature trees. The project has several important fiscal governance and environmental problems. First, the current price tag stands at $24 $4 million and rising. Still, there has been, in my opinion, an under-budgeting of maintenance costs and security challenges. To be ADA compliant, the trail will need regular clearing, and staffing for such endeavors has not yet been addressed. Similarly, security and emergency services have not been discussed in conjunction with this project. In terms of other projects, we have just invested heavily in the stream restoration, and that project entailed creation of anti-erosion measures. The George Snyder Trail will circle very close to the Akatin Creek. And this video shows flooding even without the removal of 500 mature trees that the George Snyder Trail would entail. Next, in terms of governance, there is a NEPA document filed which stated that there is no substantial controversy to the project. On the horizon. However, there is significant public opposition on record dating back to 2020, and more recently a petition with more than 3,000 signatures. There is a significant environmental impact that is well recorded with input from very specialized groups who have shared their expertise with the city. Even the city's own master plan, with its chapter on environment and sustainability, undermines the precepts of the project. Thank you to all those council members who continue to exercise fiscal restraint and environmental stewardship. And I urge you all to factor in these considerations in making your decision. Thank you. That was our last video testimony at this time. If there's anybody else in the room that would like to speak to this item, if I can ask you please line up by the window side and come up one at a time stating your name and street address and comment. Hi, I want to respect everybody's time and the passion of all that have been speaking tonight. Um, my name is Erin Mazzi, I live at 106 or 10165 Fairwoods Drive in the Mosby Woods Condominiums. So I, I rent, but I talk to my neighbors who are owners, and one of them has also asked me to quote her concerns of calling this an abomination of a plan and cost, and is highly concerned about what tearing down so much of this green space protection from noise, all the construction, is going to do to her own property. Values as well. I can't really speak outside as much of the context because this green space in this area was a large part of why I moved here. These trails and the opportunities for transit have been such an important part of why I've remained in Fairfax for the 3 years that I have since I moved to this community. And I love that— me and my husband both love that we don't drive very much already. We could walk to the CVS, we could walk and get sushi. And this is an area that already has significant access, um, and good access along the roads to reach, um, businesses and to go to these communities. And I see my neighbors taking it all the time. I hear a lot of wonderful things about building community, about the future, and I can tell you that my neighbors are already out there on these streets. They're already walking these trails. They're already using and experiencing and enjoying the nature, and this is not the correct way to move forward and to connect more, more people. It just isn't. It's— it feels more like a way to put essentially a highway through our parks and playgrounds that we already have, and including like, I I'm just baffled to see that this trail, especially with high speed, it goes next to at least 3 playgrounds along the way or straight through them as they go through. I don't— and just seeing the grade of the terrain, seeing like walking on it, like it's baffling that this is the spot to build such an extensive construction. I'm— I, again, I love these trails. I love everything that I've experienced about being in Fairfax. Fairfax, especially when it comes to transit, especially when it comes to the investment, things like Tree City. And that means a lot to me. And so far, like, we've had a wonderful experience. I'll tell you, though, that like looking out my window at the stream restoration is already pretty much almost enough to make us leave and move somewhere else. And the burden has already fallen so significantly. And it also baffles me me to hear people talk about affordable housing, to talk about business and about improvement, when there is already one of the more affordable communities here that I live in that is bearing the brunt of the environmental impacts. It is already— and it does make me doubt the sincerity of the people who say this. So thank you for all of your time. I respect it's a difficult decision, and the passion of supporters and opponents as well.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Good evening, Mayor and Council. I'm Roger Custer, 10131 Cardinal Road, and we have the privilege of backing up to the forest that is proposed to be cut down for this project. And one of the animals who lives in that forest, uh, my kids have dubbed Freddy the Fox, and we love it when he comes into our backyard and explores through Mosby Woods. But I fear that this project will be cutting down his home along with many other animals and species that we enjoy in our wonderful city. Uh, and you've heard about all the other environmental impacts. I don't need to repeat that. I'd like to urge you to consider not appropriating the money and canceling the project. And I have a unique proposal for what we can do, uh, as a city to achieve our common goals here, uh, to be a tree city, to have an urban forest, and to be proud of our wilderness and our trees, which is I think we should work together to obtain federal funds for open space. Uh, we have the Parks Foundation, which potentially could raise money I'm a professional nonprofit fundraiser, and I would personally be happy to help put together a coalition to save this forest and really make maybe even an example for other cities that are having this discussion about development or not development. And really, I think we could work together to do that, to have a positive solution here that really meets the goals of everyone who's been speaking tonight for our city. So I hope we can do that. I hope we can save Freddie the Fox and move forward with Fairfax together. Thank you very much.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Good evening. My name is Beth Ayaneda. I live at 10916 Berry Street in the Westmore community. Tonight I'm speaking on behalf of myself and in support of this trail. Um, I usually write my remarks down, but I didn't tonight, so I'm going to kind of wing it. But I've listened to a lot of— a lot of sides of the arguments, and one thing I want to point out from my perspective— I've worked for the county for 25 years. Um, I had less gray hair when I started, but I also had less perspective in terms of— this project is what is defined as a generational project. And you've seen it because of all of the opposition and support and multiple perspectives that come forward here. And my experience from being a transportation planner was the I-66 project presenting the opportunity to provide those connections on a highway project with pedestrian and bicycle facilities built into the infrastructure. One thing people don't know is that VDOT was only required to build sidewalks to consider sidewalks as part of their projects in 2004, not 1960. It only became a policy of theirs in 2004. And so that's why it's taken so long to get to the point where ped and bikes are considered a priority to outweigh the vehicles that have taken over in terms of priority. So this project came at a time when they did consider a ped and bike facility that has already been built. Additional signals for crossings on 123 have been built to tie into this project. Things have been put in place more than just a Comp Plan, but other infrastructure to support this project. I, for one, didn't originally come from Virginia, but I moved here after school being a Virginia Tech. And I come from Rhode Island, a very tiny state, and so a very tight-knit community. But Fairfax City has been my home, and I found that those same values here. One thing that I do want to point out is that, you know, these opportunities in this little city of 6 square miles of this infrastructure don't come along very often. And it creates a legacy that you can be a part of, or you can also be part of the legacy of its demise of this project. So please take all that into consideration, not just the impacts, but really the kind of the softer human parts that come into it in terms of being infrastructure, the opportunities that are here, being connected. So I support this project. I also support that the CCT be rerouted to be part of the City of Fairfax and not along Blake Lane, which is what this project would also help provide. So there is a bigger context here, and hopefully you can see that, the forest through the trees. Thank you very much. Talia Schultz. I live on University City Drive in City of Fairfax. I have questions. I know we can't discuss them in my 3 minutes, but they seem worth asking. The cost estimates are already over budget even though the project hasn't broken ground. What happens if construction is underway and it goes over budget further? This seems likely considering increasing costs that we are all experiencing. Part of this project is going through a floodplain, removing trees whose roots stabilize the floodplain and also help soak up water. 100-year floods are becoming more frequent. Assuming this is built, how much money has been allocated to maintenance, repairs from flooding, and stream restoration? We've also been talking about how we can just plant saplings to replace the mature trees. There was an article in the DCist saying local forests are failing to provide the next generation of trees. All along the East Coast. In the D.C. area, 9 out of the 11 parks studied were in imminent or probable failure category. They urge preserving the existing forests. I actually went through and Google Maps and put the city boundaries to find out like where the— what forests do we have in this area. And really other than Daniels Run Park and what we have at Van Dyke Park, park. This is very developed. So I can give you copies of that. I'm also unclear how it's good for business to direct people through a forest. Doesn't seem like you can shop well there. And my— I'm not against having a connecting path. This is not an easy decision. My concern is this project as proposed could end up being an ongoing money pit, more than the $3.7 million already discussed, as it encounters further unexpected costs. So thank you. Hello, um, I'm Jennifer Gear, and I live at 10013 Orchard Court. And I'm calling here to— I don't have prepared remarks, but I'm here to speak against this particular project. I'm all for development in the city, in particular projects that benefit a lot of the residents. The Thais Park project is fabulous. That's a great place for our children, you know, to gather and, and, and for families to meet and, and be involved in sports. We have a lot of great trails in the city, all behind Daniel's Mosey Woods Run. I personally love to walk behind there on the Country Club Hills Park trails. Those are fabulous trails. The ideas being presented about this trail, that it will connect people to the businesses, makes no sense to me. The, the Mosey Woods community is far away from our downtown area and a lot of our businesses where people would be going by foot. We have a lot of great areas to walk in the city. Perhaps we need to focus on making sure that all of our streets have sidewalks, because I live in a neighborhood that does not have sidewalks on all of the streets, so residents have to walk on the road. I know we're studying projects to add more sidewalks, but if we want to connect the city, we need to focus on the sidewalks, focus on the larger projects that most of our residents can use. I don't recall ever seeing any type of survey sent out to residents asking, is this a project that we want? Is this how we want our tax dollars to be spent? This would not be something I ever would As I said, I'm all for improving all of our parks, Radcliffe Park, the park that— the Thais Park, of course, any of the different parks with the playgrounds. I think we need to work on those. But a trail that we don't know how many people are going to use, to be honest with you, I have seen very few bike riders around the city. We may have some people that are biking enthusiasts and there's lots of trail— of bike paths for people to ride all the way down to D.C. We can have an opportunity through other paths in Fairfax to offer this type of mode of transportation for people that want it. But the majority of the people to me in the city, they tend to walk. So I think that this is a project that's very costly. Environmentally, it's absolutely horrible. I grew up in Ohio, and we had lots of woods and trails. I spent a lot of time there as a young person. And it would be really horrible to see the devastation to the forest, A lot of people, great people, have spoken about the environmental impact, so I won't go back into that again. But I wanted to again come in person to express my request to please vote no on this particular project. The money does not— it appears it may not have to be paid back. That should not be a reason why we move forward on a project like this. Thank you. Good evening. Shannon Daniels. I'm 3800 Maud Street. Didn't prepare to speak tonight, but want to just ask you all for your strength and discipline in this situation. I think we arrived at a point where we knew we would need this funding, and we had some good ideas for alternatives. I'm asking for your strength. Thank you. Hi, I'm Val Morgan, 3617 Cornell Road. In those pictures that Tom gave you, I'm one of the posts. And instead of just doing something for your dear selfish self, there's a lot of living things that you're going to kill. All the trees, all the birds, they can't, they can't live just with no trees. There's some great things that don't need to die. And there's some ironwood trees. Those are gorgeous trees and very angular and different. And they're precious. We should just save all the living things that are there. Thanks. I'm Katie Johnson, 10132 Spring Lake Terrace. Cardinal Road, Park, Draper Drive already exist, already connected. Cardinal Road is slated to become a neighborway, which is— has been identified as a safe, underutilized neighborhood street. I have worked adjacent to this forest for the last 34 years. I have spent literally thousands of hours in these woods. I have heard the sounds of frogs, so many that I would run outside every time it rained to watch the Cope's gray tree frogs climb the walls. I must echo the alarms of Rachel Carlson. I have not seen or heard a Cope's gray tree frog in many years. They are harbingers of what is to come. Much of this trail already exists. This is in perspective. This is less than 2 miles, 1.78, less than a 10-minute bike ride for $24.7 million. Please do not appropriate the funds. Hi, good evening. No, you don't have to stick to what they said, and they even recognized that at the time, and, and rightly so. We're, we're concerned about that. The city did not sign any contract or make a commitment to repay expended concession funds. There is no such thing as a binding contract on this project. It'd actually be great if there were, because then we would know what the repayment terms are, if there are any, wouldn't we? Moreover, in the MOA that governs the concession fund in general, there is an option in black and white to actually cancel if a project comes in over budget. VDOT doesn't want you to build this just to avoid some threat of repayment. In fact, it doesn't want you to build it or not build it. It's your decision. They're not going to put the thumb on the scale by threatening repayment. They will help you once you make the decision to figure out what needs to be repaid. That's their job. That's what they told me. It's not— all these histrionics about payback, I think, are overwhelming you. Thank you. Hello, I'm Susan Keiler, 9742 Cardinal. I was not going to comment tonight until residents forwarded me some emails from an elected official that contained misinformation about the George Snyder Trail, and still I wasn't going to comment until the elected official posted the same misinformation on, on Facebook. I will address only the salient points now that have not been previously noted by others, I hope, and the other points offline, because you cannot build anything on misinformation. It is not true that the George Snyder Trail is, quote, has been part of a regional trail connectivity plan since the 1990s, unquote. It was strictly a city affair and a walking trail and not a biking trail. Yes, there have been years of delay that increased construction costs. Everything is increasing in cost, and the city's taking of the private property for GST has taken a very long time. However, whether the city has a strong record of delivering multi-million dollar projects on time and on budget is debatable. Let's look at the timeline. 2015 to 2017, VDOT did not approve the GST for SmartScale funding because it did not meet transportation priorities. Using a much lower bar than SmartScale, NVTA recommends and CTB approves the GST for concession funds. The GST estimated cost is $13.96 million, of which $360,000 is federal money. Also in 2017, the city issues its first multimodal plan, which shows the GST as a proposed trail. It does not include the GST as a connection to the Cross County Trail. 2020, the GST design is modified to include bridges. The cost increases to $17.6 million and the federal portion increases to $300,000.95. 2002, GST design is modified to comply with federal ADA accessible requirements and to connect to the cross-county trail. The cost increases to $20.6 million. 2025, the lower of the two bids comes in higher than funds appropriated and the cost increases to $24. $1.9 million for a 1.7-mile trail to accommodate the difference and add project management and other costs. Yes, residents value environmental, health, and community benefits of accessible trails, but not in the abstract. Not when the trail will be built on floodplains and in resource protection areas in violation of the Chesapeake Bay Act. The state is spending millions of dollars to preserve our wild spaces. The county has said transportation projects should only be built in RPAs as a last resort when there is no other alternative to meet the pressing need. There are alternatives to the GST, including using existing roads, but they were not explored by the City. No, the $9.5 million grant for the GST expansion extension is not at risk if the GST does not proceed. The extension improves Fairfax Boulevard, so the project can proceed with or without the GST. Canceling a questionable project, an unnecessary project, will likely enhance and not, as it is asserted, jeopardize the credibility. It would be irresponsible to build the GSG on misinformation. Thank you. Good evening and happy New Year. I know it's pretty tough for each one of you sitting here to make that decision, and I stand here as— obviously I had to state my name. So I've been teaching for 30 years now as a science teacher, and I support, I support trails. That's not my reason to stand here today. We need to— I have a question. What is a trail? I think we have lost the definition of a trail. Is trail to preserve the wooded trees and path and go through that, or destroy it and make a concrete where Does it serve the purpose? Will the people stop going to work using their cars and start biking in this if we make this concrete with the destruction? I have— we have got this money from VDOT. Yes, we have got a gift. We take it, we lose it. We take that money. Yes. But what are the future maintenance of this. We have approved— the council before has approved stormwater utility fee. Why? Because it was an issue in the city, and it is an issue, and the cost of maintaining everything is only going up and up, right? This maintenance of this is only going to go up. Will it create more runoff? I don't know the answer. Experts can tell me, but my science background says yes, it will. If we do deforestation, it will increase erosion, runoff. And yes, now we will increase the stormwater utility fee. City citizens are already hurting with the cost of living going up. Is this a need or is this a want? Do we have to— if we don't do this trail, will we be really worse? Will we really destroy the city? Or are we leaving the trees and protecting the environment for the future generation to come, as our forefathers left for us? That's the question for you all today. Thank you. Is there anybody else in the room that would like to speak? To speak on this item? Okay. Is— yes, we'll have a 5-minute recess. We'll be back in 5 minutes. All right, we are back, and I would like at this time to close the public hearing. Is there a motion? Councilmember Hardy Chandler. I move to approve the supplemental appropriation resolution in the amount of $4,000,000 $600,000 from Transform 66 Outside the Beltway concession funding for the George Snyder Trail project. Second. A motion has been made by Councilmember Hardy Chandler and seconded by Councilmember Amos. Is there any discussion on the motion? I'm going to guess that there probably is. Councilmember Bates.

turns.text: Thank you. Um, so I've spoken before about the fact that this project would produce a, um, uh, shared use path compliant with state guidelines where one does not exist right now along, uh, what is perhaps one of, if not the most busy corridors in the city. And it would provide a direct connection to the Jerry Connolly Cross County Trail via the Wilcoxon Trail and the George Snyder Extension Project, which is a separate project that has been funded and is not simply designating sidewalks as shared use paths. It is constructing shared use paths along Fairfax Boulevard from this project to the Wilcoxon Trail with a crossing at the crosswalk. Now, I have never supported cancelling the project, but I have supported building only the portion west of Fairwoods Parkway. Go back and change things after the January 2024 vote. And this is how long I've been voting on this project. This is how long this decision has been before us. It wasn't something that was just sprung on us over the course of a couple months. It's been before us, um, you know, most of the time I've been on the council. Um, on this, uh, the 20— the 2024 vote on this option failed. I would have pushed forward with only the full trail. I have been so disappointed by much of the rhetoric surrounding this project, and particularly by the article that was retroactively designated an op-ed by the publisher despite it appearing on the front page with no such label or disclosure of the recent candidacy of one of the authors, which heavily focused on this very project. The article falsely claimed that our staff had misled us regarding the project agreement, from which it took a small section out of context. In which I read a more extensive excerpt from word for word last meeting. I have complete faith in the integrity and competence of our staff, and I've seen their dedication and objectivity firsthand. There is no place in civic discourse for baseless attacks on our staff and our neighbors when we want the best for our city.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Um, other comments? Councilmember McQuillan. This is a very difficult vote, obviously, for all of us. It's not one that I take lightly. I've spent significant time reviewing the record, the data, the long-term implications of this decision. I support trails, I support connectivity and accessible infrastructure. But I also have a responsibility to evaluate whether a project, as currently designed and priced, remains the most responsible choice for our community. This past Saturday, I visited the future trail alignment in person during active rainfall. I wanted to observe the stormwater runoff conditions following the recent stream restoration. Seeing the site under real conditions reinforce the environmental maintenance and safety complex— complexities associated with this project. The George Schneider Trail concept dates back to the early 2000s and was incorporated into the city's 2035 Comprehensive Plan. I know this because I went back and watched every single video on our city website archive page that had anything to do with George Schneider Trail. I wanted to see for myself from the very beginning what led to this point. Over more than a decade, it has undergone repeated study, redesign, and public discussion. By September 8th, 2020, the project had reached 60% design. At that meeting, council members Harmon, Lim, Yee, Miller, and Ross all raised concerns about environmental impacts, safety, maintenance backlog, and rapidly increasing costs, which were already approaching roughly $7 million per mile based on the design at that time. This was never a simple endorsement but acknowledgment of tradeoffs. When bids were received— I'm sorry, subsequent revisions reduced the project footprint and lowered estimated tree impacts from 42% to 28%. However, those changes added complexity, including additional bridges, boardwalks, grading, and alignment modifications. As a result, total project costs increased to $17.6 million. When bids were received in 2025, they came in roughly $4.6 million over the available funding. One construction— once construction management VDOT oversight and contingency costs are included, the total project cost now exceeds $20 million. While the exact per-mile figure varies depending on how costs are allocated, what's clear is the cost per mile has increased substantially from early estimates. City staff deserve credit for securing significant outside funding, including more than $13.6 million from I-66 outside beltway concessionaire funds and CMAQ allocations. That work has been strong and diligent. However, tonight's decision is not just about construction costs. ADA-compliant shared use paths require ongoing operational commitments, including debris removal, vegetation management, snow and ice clearing, surface upkeep, inspections, and liability mitigation. Available data suggests annual maintenance costs beginning around $25,000 and increasing over time, creating permanent obligations in our operating budget that compete with public safety, stormwater, sidewalks, and core services. This project has also required multiple additional funding requests. In mid-2024, an additional $3 million was requested as cost estimates increased. Now an additional $4.6 million is required to award the current bid. Repeated cost escalation is a signal we cannot ignore. This vote is not a rejection of trails or accessibility. It is a vote for responsible planning and right-sized solutions. Connectivity can be improved through lower cost, faster to implement alternatives, One such option, which I have not heard at all through this entire discussion, is amending our sidewalk ordinance to explicitly allow cyclists to use sidewalks in areas where roadway conditions are hazardous or unsafe. This approach is already used in many jurisdictions and would require minimal funding. It could be implemented quickly and would provide meaningful east-west connectivity without committing the city to a highly engineered project with escalating capital and long-term operational costs. Responsible governance requires us to reassess when facts change. Cost escalation, environmental complexity, and long-term obligations matter. We owe it to current residents and future generations to weigh those realities carefully. For this reason— for these reasons, I will not be supporting appropriating funds for this project. Councilmember Amos.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: No, I, I appreciate it, and I'll just be brief. I wanted to say thank you to everyone who spoke. Obviously, this is a very passionate subject. Uh, nice crash course for our new city manager. Welcome to your, uh, your first official meeting. Um, when I first heard about the trail, which was 2024, uh, you know, I was like, oh, it's a trail, no biggie. And then next thing I know, it stuck around for quite a while until It turns out there were a lot of interested parties. And granted, from where I grew up in Germany, for example, we have trails everywhere, often around the size of this. It's not a shocker. Um, Hawaii, there's a lot of natural trails where you don't see trails as extensive as this one or as large. Um, and so it's been an interesting perspective to listen to the community, hear your feedback, and it's a rare, rare topic where everyone is to some degree correct. And I think we tend to forget that you can like trails, you can even like this trail, but acknowledge that there are environmental concerns with it. And I think that's completely fair and valid. And so I don't want that to get lost as we progress forward, because whenever this subject comes up, we tend to lose our, our neighborhood spirit, and we tend to go after one another with such vitriol, and that doesn't need to be the case. And so I initially opposed this project, and then I was actually intending on supporting the third option back in June until I heard the public testimony from both supporters and detractors who pretty much said, we don't want the third option, either go the full thing or cancel it. And so I decided to support the full thing, and I won't get into money. If you've heard me speak before, you know very well how I feel about that. Um, from a developer perspective, from my day job, which I've been in a year to today, when it comes to pro formas and financing, if I were to apply for financing from either state bonds or federal for a 9% or 4% LIHTC deal and then canceled the project after I was awarded the funds, they would have some concerns. And that's something that does rest with me. And that I have to take into consideration. So regardless of what happens, there's going to be some risk. And, you know, I hope I'm wrong. I really do. And even during this past year, there's been surveys— the community survey, park survey— and I know that part of the question is, what is a trail? That's a good question to ask. But in our community and park surveys, both of them cite that people do want trails. And that's something that we do have to contend with as a council and that we're trying to figure out. When it comes to crime and trash, which is in some of the testimony tonight, I do want to acknowledge that most, if not nearly all, studies highlight that actually trails lead to often a reduction in crime. And then with trash pickup, similarly. So while I do intend on supporting this I want us to acknowledge that there's legitimacy to everything that everyone said in this room today, but I also agree that it's time to move on. We've dealt with this long enough. We keep going at each other's throats, and personally, I think this is an unhealthy thing for our community to keep prolonging. And so let's get it done.
turns.speaker: Councilmember Anthony Amos

turns.text: Councilmember Hall. Thank you. I had a small statement prepared, but I left it on my home computer, so I'm going to do my best to just wing it and we'll go from there. I do, first of all, want to thank everyone who has sent emails over the past year, those who have come out to speak not just at this meeting but at previous meetings, those who have spoken up at budget concerns, those who have, you know, tracked me down at a coffee shop just to talk about this project, whether you're for it or against it. I do want to thank you sincerely for being an involved participant in your community, in your neighborhood, and in your city, because we don't always get that, uh, especially in other cities and towns across Virginia. Um, we had nothing like this in Delaware where I grew up. I mean, people didn't go to meetings like this, even though I'm sure they had them, but we just didn't do that. Um, so this is a whole new experience, and I think it says a lot for our community. I will also agree that this has been a huge burden and a huge thorn in the side of our community. I think it has split communities, it's split neighbors, it's split friends. You know, if it's kind of like politics, it seems, where if you're not on exactly the same page sometime at some day, it's like your friends don't want to talk to you anymore just because you might disagree on one one thing, and I refuse personally to let a trail be something that's going to separate me from people that I care about and I thought care about me. So with that, I do understand that residents want trails per our survey. I want trails, and I answered our survey, so I was one of those respondents. And when I think of a trail, and from what I've heard from many of you, is that when you think of a trail, you think of Daniels Runway. You think of a trail that runs through the woods that looks like it belongs there, that does not have these huge walls to protect you from the side of a hill or a mountain or to keep you from falling off the side of something into the stream restoration area. That to me is not a trail. By definition, I guess it may be, The fact that it requires transportation dollars to get us to this trail really makes me wonder, is it a trail or not? So, you know, what this started out as with Parks and Rec and with all of the other individuals that went into this truly was a trail. It was a stone dust trail— excuse me, stone dust trail, $200,000. And I would love to get us back to that option. Back to an option where if you want to ride a bike, you can go along Cardinal Road. You can go along a designated area that is paved and has an opportunity for bikes. If you also want to walk or hike, you have some other more rugged options. The issue of accessibility, I know, has been up— been brought up a number of times. And months ago, probably even a year ago at this point almost, I did reach out to our Parks and Rec Department and asked about the wheelchairs that you can go on in order to go into rugged terrain. And while we were away over Christmas break, one of the friends that we were with, she hurt her knee and she had to go in a wheelchair. We were at the beach. We went and we found the little tent where the rescue people were, and they put her in a wheelchair with these giant wheels and they wheeled her down to the water. And we were able to let her enjoy the beach, let her enjoy the pool, just like everybody else was. You know, it was a temporary wheelchair for her, fortunately, but from what I've heard from people that have durable equipment like that, they don't want to put it on a trail. They don't want it to be subject to getting hurt or getting harmed. So the fact that Fairfax County also is no longer doing paved trails in forest area to me is a big concern. I think that we can be a leader and say, you know what? Stop this. And it doesn't mean that we're not a good partner or not a good leader if we choose to go a different direction than you personally might think. On the flip side, I would say that that shows that maybe we're a good leader in our community supporting other voices or other ideas or other suggestions. Council members suggest— I'm sorry, Councilmember Hall— oh my gosh, Councilmember McQuillan's suggestion about the sidewalks and potentially changing the ordinance, that could be a game changer. You know, is it something everybody wants? Probably not. Will we know? No, because we don't talk to everybody, but we certainly try. I'm sure I'm rambling here, and I don't really mean to, but all I ask is that this one moment— I think I said this the last time we voted on this trail— this one moment in time should not define who you are individually, what you individually stand for. There's so much more that makes up you and our community, and I truly am ready to put this to bed, but in a different way. And so I will not be voting to support this trail. Thank you. Councilmember Hardy Chandler. Thank you. Um, I too really appreciate all of the input and engagement from the community on this, and, uh, both in terms of coming here and speaking and in terms of emails and other ways that people have expressed their positions. For me tonight, one of the most poignant times, although I listened very closely to everyone, one of the most poignant times was just hearing from the people who were here from the very beginning and talking about how they still are committed to this. After seemingly decades. For me, the question isn't whether or not this is a trail. The trail is about something much bigger. There's a saying, I believe it was Maya Angelou who said, "Don't let facts get in the way of truth." And I really believe that this is about— the trail is sort of an example of Things like promises, things like reliability, things like credibility, things like partnership, thinking larger. It's about acknowledging the history and the origins of what brought this before us. And I do believe it is about investing in the infrastructure for things that will be a part of the future. So I do echo the sentiments that there is truth in all of the positions. But for me, the trail is certainly important in and of itself, but it's more important in terms of signaling something larger than ourselves. And I feel like our council needs to be challenged to think beyond ourselves and what this means in a much broader way, both in terms of the story behind this trail, but also the future we want to build. Also in terms of governance decisions, I really think that we do have to do a better job acknowledging those who came before us on this council and certainly preparing for the decisions that future councils will make. I will be supporting the appropriation. I believe it's the right thing not only for this trail but in terms of good governance. Councilmember Peterson. Mr.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read

turns.text: Lassner. Oops. I will be brief. It is hard to really add too much to what everybody else has said. I think it is very important to thank everybody who attended, not just who came this evening, but obviously all the people who are watching and those of you who submitted video testimony. It is really great. All of that information is extremely helpful and it has been. All along the way. So I'll tell you that one of the difficulties that I had with the setup for this public hearing this evening is that it was based on the premise that we had only 4 options, and that's clearly not the case. We have a great many other options. We began a process last year to begin looking at those, very much with the agreement and encouragement of VDOT and the community. And people were genuinely excited about the opportunity to look at different approaches here that could reconcile the conflicts that so clearly exist with this project in comparison with the desires that people have to have the kind of trail you have heard talked about this evening, something very simple. And at the same time, be able to make improvements on the way we can use bikes on our streets in the city. Unfortunately, that process was terminated very quickly. To this very day, we don't know why. We did our best at the council to try to understand why it didn't happen. It's past history. We ended up with where we are today, and I think what I've heard this evening is that that has been seen as a very difficult proposition for most people in the city, and that has been the case for a very long time. I do think, um, it's time to move on, and I think that we have, um, really a lot of good reasons not only to move on here today but to look forward to other opportunities and options. I, I will just close by saying that it's a bit fitting that we will be making a decision this evening through an appropriations vote. And just a reminder that an appropriations vote ended the Vietnam War. And I think it may be time to end this war. We've been at this a long time. It's time to move on. One of my brothers was number one in the last draft of the Vietnam War. He did not go to Southeast Asia because of the Hatfield-McGovern Amendment. On an appropriation. He was greatly relieved that he didn't have to go to war, but it did not stop his patriotism and it did not stop his service to his country. He enlisted in the United States Marine Corps and he served many years. We have a very dear friend in the same situation, the exact same time, who went on to become a Navy fighter pilot, active duty, er, active duty on carriers. He served his country. He was a patriot. Neither of them wanted to go to that war. Neither of them believed in it, but they believed in their country, just as we all here believe in our community. They served because they fought— they thought there was a better way. They thought there was a better venue for them to serve. And I think there's a better venue for us all here in the community to approach our interest in trails and our stewardship. This particular course of action that we have been now looking at for a great many years does not appear to be that course. There are other courses out there. As we close tonight's meeting, I want to share an important announcement. I will not be seeking reelection as Mayor of Fairfax City in 2026. It's been an honor to serve in this role. Every day I have worked to uphold my oath of office and to serve this— serve this city to the very best of my ability. I intend to continue doing so for the remaining 351 days of my term. Sitting in this middle seat with two different city councils has given me a perspective that only 12 people in our city's history have held before me. No combination of thoughtfully considered words accurately convey or effectively communicate the complexity of the last 3 years, so I won't try. What I will say is this: our city charter is outdated and ill-equipped to meet the demands of a modern 21st century community. There have been recommendations by charter commissions in the past to make changes since its adoption, 1966, but apparently no political will to actually do so. From my vantage point of occupancy, the chair I'm sitting in is broken. As I prepare to leave the seat for someone else, I am committing myself to being an agent of change in improving our system of governance. I was active in city affairs long before becoming mayor and will continue to speak up when I am no longer in elected office. I am deeply committed to supporting those who step forward to serve our democratic institutions at the local, state, and federal levels. Now more than ever, defending our country's founding principles requires engagement and effort beyond what many of us have been asked to give before. You will see me knocking on doors in 2026 elections on behalf of candidates I support, and I invite you to engage in conversations about how we can move Fairfax City forward toward a bright, healthy, and prosperous future. I care deeply about this community. Its residents, its businesses, and the exceptional city staff whose dedication makes our quality of life the envy of this region. To my colleagues on the dais, I look forward to working together through the end of our term. And with that, we are adjourned at 11:08 on Tuesday.
turns.speaker: Mayor Catherine Read
